sections.title: Introduction to Relative Pronouns
sections.content: Relative pronouns introduce relative clauses (subordinate clauses that describe a noun). They connect the clause to its antecedent (the noun being described). The main relative pronouns are: qui, que, quoi, dont, où, lequel.
examples: Le livre qui est sur la table (The book that is on the table)
La voiture que j'ai achetée (The car that I bought)
L'endroit où je vis (The place where I live)

sections.title: Qui (Who/Which as Subject)
sections.content: Qui is used as the subject of the relative clause. It replaces the antecedent and performs the action in the clause. It does not change form for gender or number.
examples: L'homme qui parle est mon professeur (The man who is speaking is my teacher)
La femme qui chante est ma sœur (The woman who is singing is my sister)
Les enfants qui jouent sont bruyants (The children who are playing are noisy)
Le livre qui est intéressant (The book that is interesting)

sections.title: Que (Whom/Which as Direct Object)
sections.content: Que is used as the direct object of the relative clause. It replaces the antecedent which is the direct object of the verb in the clause. Que becomes qu' before a vowel.
examples: Le livre que je lis est bon (The book that I am reading is good)
La femme que tu vois est ma mère (The woman whom you see is my mother)
Les fleurs que j'ai achetées sont belles (The flowers that I bought are beautiful)
L'homme qu'elle aime (The man whom she loves)

sections.title: Dont (Of Whom/Of Which)
sections.content: Dont replaces a noun introduced by 'de' (of, from, about). It indicates possession or origin.
examples: L'homme dont je t'ai parlé (The man about whom I told you)
La voiture dont il rêve (The car of which he dreams)
Le livre dont la couverture est bleue (The book whose cover is blue)
Les gens dont j'ai besoin (The people of whom I have need)

sections.title: Où (Where/When)
sections.content: Où refers to a place or time. It replaces expressions of location or time introduced by prepositions like 'à', 'dans', 'sur', 'en', etc.
examples: La ville où je vis (The city where I live)
Le jour où nous nous sommes rencontrés (The day when we met)
L'école où il étudie (The school where he studies)
L'année où tout a changé (The year when everything changed)

sections.title: Lequel (Which)
sections.content: Lequel and its forms (laquelle, lesquels, lesquelles) are used to replace a noun + preposition when the preposition is not 'de'. They agree in gender and number with the antecedent.
examples: Le livre avec lequel j'étudie (The book with which I study)
La femme pour laquelle il travaille (The woman for whom he works)
Les outils avec lesquels il travaille (The tools with which he works)
Les raisons pour lesquelles nous sommes partis (The reasons for which we left)

sections.title: Quoi (What)
sections.content: Quoi is used to refer to inanimate things, often after a preposition. It means 'what' or 'that which'.
examples: Je ne sais pas à quoi tu penses (I don't know what you're thinking about)
Voilà de quoi j'ai besoin (Here is what I need)
C'est ce à quoi je fais référence (That's what I'm referring to)

sections.title: Practice Exercise
sections.content: Combine these pairs of sentences using relative pronouns: 1) C'est un livre. Le livre est intéressant. 2) J'ai vu un film. Le film était passionnant. 3) Voici la maison. Je vis dans cette maison. 4) C'est l'homme. L'homme a gagné le prix.
